subject: Pulmonology
question: A 22-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing, chest tightness, and dry cough, especially at night. Symptoms are triggered by cold air and exercise. PFT shows FEV1 65% predicted with 15% improvement after bronchodilator. What is the best initial maintenance therapy?

options.explanation: SABA alone is for intermittent asthma (symptoms <2x/week). This patient has persistent symptoms (nighttime, frequent). GINA no longer recommends SABA-only for safety reasons. Low-dose ICS is first-line for persistent asthma.

Persistent asthma requires daily controller medication. Low-dose ICS is first-line for mild persistent asthma. Reduces airway inflammation, prevents exacerbations. Can use ICS-formoterol as both maintenance and reliever (SMART therapy per GINA).

Montelukast (leukotriene receptor antagonist) is second-line or add-on therapy, not first-line. Less effective than ICS for airway inflammation. Used for exercise-induced or allergic asthma as adjunct. ICS first-line.
LABA monotherapy without ICS is CONTRAINDICATED in asthma due to increased risk of asthma-related death (SMART trial). LABAs must always be combined with ICS in asthma.
Omalizumab is for severe allergic asthma not controlled with high-dose ICS+LABA. Step 5 therapy. Not first-line for mild-moderate persistent asthma. Reserved for refractory cases.

educationalObjective: Initiate appropriate step-wise therapy for persistent asthma: low-dose ICS first-line. Never use LABA without ICS. SABA alone no longer recommended by GINA.
keyTakeaway: Asthma: ICS first-line for persistent symptoms. LABA only with ICS (never alone). SMART therapy (ICS-formoterol) for maintenance + reliever.
